audit_status.source_base: Greek Romans text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Paul's grief, Israel privileges, promise/election, mercy, potter imagery, remnant, nations attaining righteousness/justice, stumbling stone. Anti-Jewish use must be rejected.

verses.restored: I speak truth in Messiah; I do not lie, my conscience bearing witness together with me in holy Spirit,
that there is great grief for me and unceasing pain in my heart.
For I could pray that I myself be accursed away from Messiah for my siblings, my kin according to flesh,
who are Israelites, whose are the adoption, the glory, the covenants, the giving of the Law, the service, and the promises,
whose are the fathers, and from whom is the Messiah according to flesh, the One over all, Divine blessed into the ages. Amen.
But it is not as though the word of the Divine has fallen. For not all those from Israel are Israel,
nor because they are seed of Abraham are all children, but: In Isaac your seed will be called.
That is, not the children of flesh, these are children of the Divine, but the children of the promise are counted as seed.
For the word of promise is this: According to this season I will come, and Sarah will have a son.
And not only this, but also Rebecca, having conception from one, Isaac our father—
for though they were not yet born nor had practiced anything good or harmful, so that the purpose of the Divine according to choosing might remain, not from works but from the One calling—
it was said to her, The greater will serve the lesser.
Just as it is written: Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated.
What then shall we say? Is there injustice with the Divine? May it not be.
For to Moses he says, I will show mercy to whom I show mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion.
So then it is not of the one willing nor of the one running, but of the Divine showing mercy.
For the Scripture says to Pharaoh: For this very thing I raised you up, so that I might show in you my power, and so that my name might be announced in all the earth.
So then, whom he wills he shows mercy, and whom he wills he hardens.
You will say to me then, Why does he still find fault? For who has resisted his will?
O human, rather, who are you answering back to the Divine? Will what is formed say to the one forming it, Why did you make me this way?
Or does not the potter have authority over the clay, from the same lump to make one vessel for honor and another for dishonor?
But if the Divine, willing to show wrath and make known his power, bore with much patience vessels of wrath prepared for destruction,
and so that he might make known the riches of his glory upon vessels of mercy, which he prepared beforehand for glory,
even us whom he called, not only from Judeans but also from nations?
As also in Hosea he says: I will call the not-my-people, my people, and the not-beloved, beloved.
And it will be, in the place where it was said to them, You are not my people, there they will be called sons/children of the living Divine.
And Isaiah cries out concerning Israel: Though the number of the children of Israel be as the sand of the sea, the remnant will be saved/healed,
for the Lord will complete and cut short the word upon the earth.
And just as Isaiah said beforehand: Unless Lord Sabaoth had left us seed, we would have become like Sodom and been made like Gomorrah.
What then shall we say? That nations not pursuing righteousness/justice attained righteousness/justice, but righteousness/justice from trust;
but Israel, pursuing a Law of righteousness/justice, did not arrive at that Law.
Why? Because not from trust but as from works. They stumbled over the stone of stumbling,
just as it is written: Look, I place in Zion a stone of stumbling and rock of offense, and the one trusting upon him will not be put to shame.

safety_note: Romans 9 must not be used as replacement theology or anti-Jewish rhetoric. Paul begins with grief and honor for Israel's gifts, and the argument continues through Romans 10-11 toward mercy, not erasure.
